questions.question:
The village was nestled in a green spot, __________ the ocean and the hills.

A. through  B. in  C. at  D. between
questions.to_find: correct preposition to complete the sentence about the village's location

understand.plain: We need to pick the preposition that correctly describes a spatial relationship — the village is positioned in a spot that is flanked on two sides, by the ocean and the hills. The word 'between' is used when something is located in the space that separates two specific things. 'Through', 'in', and 'at' do not capture this two-sided sandwiching relationship.

keywords.explain: Used when something is in the middle of two distinct entities; indicates a position with one thing on each side.
keywords.example: The river flows between the two mountains.

keywords.explain: To nestle means to settle comfortably in a sheltered or surrounded position; hints that the location is enclosed or framed by other features.
keywords.example: The cottage was nestled between the forest and the lake.

given.aim: Choose the preposition that best shows the village is positioned with the ocean on one side and hills on the other

terms.meaning: in the space that separates two or more named things
terms.example: The town is between the river and the highway.
terms.connects: directly pairs the village's location with two specific geographic features

terms.meaning: moving from one side to the other, or across
terms.example: The road runs through the village.
terms.connects: implies motion or passage, not a static enclosed location

terms.meaning: indicates a general point or location without implying surrounding features
terms.example: The meeting is at the town hall.
terms.connects: too vague; does not capture the two-sided geographic framing

given.plan: Identify that the sentence describes a static, two-sided spatial relationship. 'Between' is the only option that captures the idea of the village being sandwiched by the ocean and the hills.

steps.title: Analyse the spatial context
steps.apply: The sentence says the village is in a green spot with two landmarks — ocean and hills. This signals a location framed by two distinct entities.
steps.note: The presence of two landmarks (ocean AND hills) connected by 'and' is the key clue.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: 'Through' implies passage or motion. 'In' lacks specificity about adjacent features. 'At' denotes a point without surrounding context. None of these link the village to both the ocean and the hills simultaneously.
steps.note: 'Between' is exclusively used for two (or more) named entities flanking a location.

steps.title: Select the correct preposition
steps.apply: 'Between the ocean and the hills' correctly conveys that the village sits in the space bounded by these two geographic features.
steps.note: Rule: use 'between' with two or a few distinct items; 'among' with a larger or indefinite group.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is used; this question tests knowledge of spatial prepositions in English.

questions.question:
While I agree _____ his proposal this time, I do not often agree ______ him.

A. to, with  B. with, to  C. with, with  D. to, to
questions.to_find: correct pair of prepositions to complete both blanks in the sentence

understand.plain: In English, the verb 'agree' takes different prepositions depending on what follows it. 'Agree to something' (a proposal, plan, or decision) means to accept or consent to it. 'Agree with someone' (a person) means to share the same opinion. Here the first blank follows 'agree' before a proposal and the second before a person (him).

keywords.explain: Used when accepting or consenting to a thing — a plan, proposal, or request.
keywords.example: She agreed to the terms of the contract.

keywords.explain: Used when sharing or matching the opinion, view, or stance of a person.
keywords.example: I agree with you on this matter.

given.aim: Fill in the two blanks with the correct prepositions for 'agree' depending on whether it is followed by a thing or a person

terms.meaning: to consent or accept a proposal, plan, or course of action
terms.example: The committee agreed to the budget.
terms.connects: first blank — 'his proposal' is a thing, so 'to' is correct

terms.meaning: to have the same opinion as someone
terms.example: I do not often agree with my colleague.
terms.connects: second blank — 'him' is a person, so 'with' is correct

terms.meaning: the fixed pairing of a verb with a specific preposition, memorised as a chunk
terms.example: depend on, rely on, agree to/with, disagree with
terms.connects: the correct answer depends on knowing the collocations of 'agree'

given.plan: Check what follows each blank. Blank 1 is before 'his proposal' (a thing) => 'to'. Blank 2 is before 'him' (a person) => 'with'.

steps.title: Identify what follows each blank
steps.apply: Blank 1 comes before 'his proposal' (a noun phrase denoting a thing). Blank 2 comes before 'him' (a pronoun denoting a person).
steps.note: Recognising thing vs. person after 'agree' is the deciding factor.

steps.title: Apply the collocation rules
steps.apply: agree + to + [plan/proposal/decision] => 'agree to his proposal'. agree + with + [person] => 'do not often agree with him'.
steps.note: 'Agree with a proposal' also exists in informal use but in standard written English 'agree to a proposal' is the accepted formal collocation.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: Blank 1 = to, Blank 2 = with => Option A (to, with).
steps.note: Option B reverses the pair; Options C and D repeat the same preposition for both blanks.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is used; this question tests collocation knowledge for the verb 'agree'.

questions.question:
The growth rate of ABC Motors in 2017 was the same ________ XYZ Motors in 2016.

A. as off  B. as those of  C. as that off  D. as that of
questions.to_find: correct phrase to complete the comparison between two companies' growth rates

understand.plain: The sentence compares a singular noun ('the growth rate of ABC Motors') with the equivalent attribute of another company. The comparison structure requires 'as ... as', and the pronoun must agree with a singular noun (growth rate is singular => 'that', not 'those'). The preposition 'of' (not 'off') connects 'that' to 'XYZ Motors'.

keywords.explain: Standard structure for equality: 'the same [noun] as [reference]'; the reference item can be replaced by a pronoun to avoid repetition.
keywords.example: Her salary is as high as that of her manager.

keywords.explain: 'That' is used as a substitute for a singular noun; 'those' for a plural noun.
keywords.example: The speed of train A is faster than that of train B (singular: speed).

given.aim: Select the phrase that correctly compares the singular noun 'growth rate' using a pronoun and the correct preposition

terms.meaning: pronoun 'that' referring to the previously mentioned singular noun, followed by 'of' to link it to its owner
terms.example: The revenue of Company A was higher than that of Company B.
terms.connects: 'growth rate' is singular, so 'that of XYZ Motors' correctly replaces 'the growth rate of XYZ Motors'

terms.meaning: 'of' is the preposition showing possession/association; 'off' means away from or separated — a different word entirely
terms.example: The lid of the box fell off.
terms.connects: options A and C use 'off' (wrong spelling/word); only D has 'of' (correct)

terms.meaning: 'those' replaces a plural noun; wrong here because 'growth rate' is singular
terms.example: The profits of both firms are higher than those of their rivals.
terms.connects: option B uses 'those' which would imply a plural antecedent — mismatch

given.plan: Step 1: check the antecedent ('growth rate' is singular => use 'that'). Step 2: check the preposition ('of', not 'off'). Only option D satisfies both conditions.

steps.title: Identify the antecedent noun
steps.apply: The noun being compared is 'the growth rate' — a singular countable noun. The replacement pronoun must therefore be 'that' (not 'those', which is plural).
steps.note: Options B uses 'those', which is incorrect for a singular noun.

steps.title: Choose the right preposition: 'of' not 'off'
steps.apply: The phrase 'as that of XYZ Motors' means 'as the growth rate of XYZ Motors'. The word needed is the preposition 'of' (showing association). 'Off' is an entirely different word.
steps.note: Options A and C use 'off' — a common spelling trap in GATE verbal questions.

steps.title: Confirm option D
steps.apply: Full sentence: 'The growth rate of ABC Motors in 2017 was the same as that of XYZ Motors in 2016.' This is grammatically correct and idiomatically natural.
steps.note: This structure ('the same ... as that of') is standard for formal comparison in English.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is used; this question tests pronoun-antecedent agreement and preposition usage in comparative structures.

questions.question:
Hema Das was _____ only Indian athlete to win _______ gold for India.

A. the, many  B. the, a  C. an, a  D. an, the
questions.to_find: correct pair of articles to fill both blanks in the sentence about Hema Das

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of definite and indefinite articles. 'The only' is a fixed collocation — 'the' must precede 'only' when it singles out one specific person. The second blank requires 'a' because winning 'a gold' means winning one (unspecified) gold medal, not a particular pre-identified one.

keywords.explain: 'The only' is a standard fixed phrase used to identify a uniquely singled-out entity. 'An only' is not used in this context.
keywords.example: She was the only student to score full marks.

keywords.explain: 'A gold' means one gold medal (first mention, non-specific); 'the gold' would refer to a specific, already-mentioned gold medal.
keywords.example: She won a gold at the Asian Games.

given.aim: Determine the correct articles for 'the/an only Indian athlete' and 'a/the gold'

terms.meaning: used before a noun that is unique, already known, or specifically identified; always used with 'only'
terms.example: She is the first person to climb the mountain.
terms.connects: blank 1 — 'the only' is the required collocation

terms.meaning: used before a singular countable noun on its first mention or when it is one of many; does not point to a specific item
terms.example: He scored a century in the match.
terms.connects: blank 2 — winning 'a gold' (one gold medal, unspecified) is the natural phrasing

terms.meaning: in English, 'only' is always preceded by 'the' when used as a pre-modifier of a noun to mean uniquely one
terms.example: She is the only candidate who passed.
terms.connects: eliminates options C and D which incorrectly use 'an' before 'only'

given.plan: Check blank 1 using the fixed phrase 'the only'. Check blank 2 using the first-mention indefinite article rule. This gives (the, a) => Option B.

steps.title: Apply the 'the only' rule to blank 1
steps.apply: 'The only' is a fixed collocation in English. Using 'an only' is ungrammatical here. So blank 1 must be 'the'. This eliminates options C and D.
steps.note: 'An only child' is a separate fixed phrase, but 'an only athlete' is not standard.

steps.title: Choose 'a' or 'the' for blank 2
steps.apply: 'Win a gold for India' uses the indefinite article because 'gold' (short for gold medal) is introduced for the first time without any prior specific reference. 'The gold' would require prior mention of a specific gold medal.
steps.note: Option A uses 'many' which doesn't work with the singular structure 'to win __ gold'.

steps.title: Confirm option B
steps.apply: 'Hema Das was the only Indian athlete to win a gold for India.' This reads fluently and is grammatically correct.
steps.note: Hema Das was the first Indian female track-and-field athlete to win gold at the Asian Games (2018).

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is used; this question tests the use of definite and indefinite articles and the fixed collocation 'the only'.

questions.question:
The boat arrived ______ dawn.

A. in  B. at  C. on  D. under
questions.to_find: correct preposition to use with the time expression 'dawn'

understand.plain: In English, time prepositions follow strict rules. 'At' is used with precise points in time or named time-of-day expressions such as dawn, dusk, noon, midnight, night. 'In' is used with longer periods (months, seasons, years, parts of the day when saying 'in the morning'). 'On' is used with dates and days. 'Under' does not apply to time at all.

keywords.explain: 'At' is used before specific time points and named moments: at dawn, at dusk, at noon, at midnight, at sunrise.
keywords.example: The birds begin to sing at dawn.

keywords.explain: Use 'at' for clock times and specific daily markers; 'in' for periods (morning/afternoon/evening when non-specific); 'on' for days and dates.
keywords.example: at 6 pm, at midnight, at dawn; in the morning; on Monday, on 5 July

given.aim: Select the preposition that correctly precedes the time-of-day expression 'dawn'

terms.meaning: the first appearance of light in the sky before sunrise; treated as a precise moment in English
terms.example: We set off at dawn.
terms.connects: as a precise named time-of-day marker, 'dawn' collocates with 'at'

terms.meaning: used with specific points in time, clock times, and named daily moments
terms.example: at noon, at midnight, at dusk, at dawn
terms.connects: the correct preposition for 'dawn'

terms.meaning: used with longer periods — months, years, centuries, seasons, or general parts of the day preceded by 'the'
terms.example: in the morning, in winter, in 2019
terms.connects: would be 'in the morning', not 'in dawn' — 'in dawn' is ungrammatical

given.plan: Recall that 'dawn' is a specific point in time (like noon or midnight) => use 'at'. Options C and D are clearly wrong. Option A ('in') would need 'the morning', not 'dawn'.

steps.title: Classify 'dawn' as a specific time point
steps.apply: 'Dawn' refers to a specific moment (first light) rather than a long period. In English, specific time markers like dawn, dusk, noon, midnight, and sunrise always use 'at'.
steps.note: Compare: 'in the morning' (general period) vs 'at dawn' (specific moment).

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply: 'In dawn' is ungrammatical. 'On dawn' is used for days, not time-of-day expressions. 'Under dawn' makes no sense in this context.
steps.note: Only 'at' fits: 'The boat arrived at dawn.'

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is used; this question tests the rule that 'at' precedes specific daily time markers such as dawn, dusk, noon, and midnight.

questions.question:
The passengers were angry ______ the airline staff about the delay.

A. on  B. about  C. with  D. towards
questions.to_find: correct preposition to follow 'angry' when expressing anger directed at a person

understand.plain: The adjective 'angry' collocates with different prepositions depending on whether the target is a person or a thing/situation. 'Angry with someone' means you feel anger directed at a person. 'Angry about something' refers to the cause or subject of anger. Here the target is 'the airline staff' (people), so 'with' is correct.

keywords.explain: 'Angry with' is used when the object of anger is a person or group of people.
keywords.example: She was angry with her brother for being late.

keywords.explain: 'Angry about' is used when the object of anger is a situation, event, or thing.
keywords.example: He was angry about the cancellation.

given.aim: Select the preposition that correctly links 'angry' to the airline staff (a group of people)

terms.meaning: used when the focus of anger is a person or group of people
terms.example: The teacher was angry with the students.
terms.connects: 'the airline staff' are people => 'angry with the airline staff'

terms.meaning: used when the focus of anger is a situation, issue, or event
terms.example: I am angry about the poor service.
terms.connects: the delay is the cause (addressed separately by 'about the delay'), not the target of anger

terms.meaning: in informal/American English, 'angry at' can refer to a person, but 'angry with' is preferred in formal and standard usage
terms.example: She was angry at the decision.
terms.connects: 'angry on' and 'angry towards' are not standard collocations in this context

given.plan: Identify that 'the airline staff' are people. 'Angry with [person]' is the standard formal collocation. 'About' refers to the cause/reason (the delay is already handled by 'about the delay' later in the sentence).

steps.title: Identify the target of the anger
steps.apply: The blank precedes 'the airline staff', who are people. When 'angry' is directed at people, the standard preposition is 'with'.
steps.note: The sentence also contains 'about the delay' — 'about' already describes the reason, not the target.

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply: 'Angry on' is not a standard collocation. 'Angry about' fits reasons/situations, not people as targets here. 'Angry towards' implies direction of movement rather than an emotional attitude in standard formal English.
steps.note: 'Angry with' is the accepted GATE-level collocation for anger directed at a person.

steps.title: Confirm answer
steps.apply: 'The passengers were angry with the airline staff about the delay.' — grammatically correct and idiomatic.
steps.note: Pattern: angry with [person] + about [reason].

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is used; this question tests the collocation 'angry with [person]' versus 'angry about [thing]'.

questions.question:
"You are delaying the completion of the task. Send _________ contributions at the earliest."

A. you are  B. your  C. you're  D. yore
questions.to_find: correct form of the possessive pronoun to complete the sentence

understand.plain: The blank precedes the noun 'contributions', so a possessive adjective (pronoun) is needed to show that the contributions belong to the person being addressed. 'Your' is the possessive form of 'you'. The options 'you are' and 'you're' are both subject+verb forms (a contraction). 'Yore' is an archaic word meaning 'long ago' and is entirely unrelated.

keywords.explain: 'Your' is the possessive form of 'you' used before a noun to show ownership or association.
keywords.example: Please submit your assignment by Friday.

keywords.explain: 'You're' is a contraction of 'you are'. It cannot be placed directly before a noun as a modifier.
keywords.example: You're late again. (= You are late again.)

given.aim: Identify that a possessive adjective is required before the noun 'contributions'

terms.meaning: possessive adjective/pronoun of 'you'; placed before a noun to indicate ownership
terms.example: Your ideas are welcome.
terms.connects: blank precedes 'contributions' (a noun) => possessive adjective needed => 'your'

terms.meaning: contraction of 'you are'; a subject-verb combination that cannot directly precede a noun as a modifier
terms.example: You're the best candidate.
terms.connects: 'You're contributions' is ungrammatical — you cannot say 'You are contributions'

terms.meaning: old English/archaic noun meaning 'a long time ago'; has no possessive or pronoun function
terms.example: knights of yore
terms.connects: entirely wrong word class and meaning; placed as a distractor

given.plan: Ask: what word can go directly before a noun to show who the contributions belong to? That is a possessive adjective. The possessive of 'you' is 'your'. All other options are either contractions, verb phrases, or unrelated words.

steps.title: Identify the grammatical role of the blank
steps.apply: The blank precedes the noun 'contributions'. The word in the blank must function as a determiner/possessive adjective. Possessive adjectives modify nouns directly.
steps.note: Pattern: ___ + noun => needs a possessive adjective.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: 'Your contributions' — correct (possessive + noun). 'You are contributions' — ungrammatical. 'You're contributions' — same problem (contraction of you are). 'Yore contributions' — archaic and meaningless here.
steps.note: Options A and C are homophones/similar sounds to 'your' — classic distractor pattern.

steps.title: Select option B
steps.apply: 'Send your contributions at the earliest.' — grammatically perfect and semantically complete.
steps.note: 'At the earliest' means as soon as possible — a formal fixed phrase used in official communications.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is used; this question tests the distinction between the possessive adjective 'your' and the contraction 'you're'.

questions.question:
The people _______________ were at the demonstration were from all sections of society.

A. whose  B. which  C. who  D. whom
questions.to_find: correct relative pronoun to introduce the relative clause modifying 'people'

understand.plain: We need a relative pronoun to introduce a clause that identifies which people are meant. The antecedent is 'people' (humans). 'Who' is used as the subject of the relative clause. 'Whom' is used as the object. 'Whose' shows possession. 'Which' is for things, not people. In the clause '__ were at the demonstration', the relative pronoun is the subject of 'were' => use 'who'.

keywords.explain: 'Who' introduces a relative clause and acts as the subject of that clause. It refers to people.
keywords.example: The scientist who discovered penicillin was Alexander Fleming.

keywords.explain: 'Whom' is used when the relative pronoun is the object of a verb or preposition within the relative clause, not the subject.
keywords.example: The person whom you called has arrived.

given.aim: Choose the relative pronoun that correctly refers to 'people' and acts as the subject of the embedded relative clause

terms.meaning: relative pronoun for people when it is the subject of the relative clause
terms.example: The teachers who attended the meeting shared their views.
terms.connects: antecedent is 'people' (human) and the pronoun is the subject of 'were' => 'who'

terms.meaning: relative pronoun for people when it is the object of the verb or preposition in the relative clause
terms.example: The candidate whom they selected was experienced.
terms.connects: here the pronoun is the subject (not object) of 'were' => 'whom' is wrong

terms.meaning: relative pronoun used for things and animals (not people) in formal written English
terms.example: The report which was submitted yesterday has errors.
terms.connects: antecedent is 'people' (human) => 'which' is incorrect

given.plan: Check 1: antecedent is human ('people') => eliminates 'which'. Check 2: the pronoun is the subject of 'were' in the relative clause => use 'who' (not 'whom'). Check 3: no possession is implied => 'whose' is wrong.

steps.title: Identify the antecedent and its type
steps.apply: The antecedent is 'the people' — a group of humans. For human antecedents, the relative pronouns are 'who', 'whom', or 'whose'. 'Which' is eliminated.
steps.note: In informal English 'that' also works, but it is not among the options.

steps.title: Determine the grammatical role of the blank
steps.apply: The relative clause is '________ were at the demonstration'. The blank is followed by the verb 'were' — so the pronoun is the subject of this verb. Subject form => 'who'. Object form ('whom') is only used when the pronoun is the object.
steps.note: Quick test: substitute 'they were at the demonstration' — 'they' is a subject pronoun => use 'who'.

steps.title: Eliminate 'whose'
steps.apply: 'Whose' implies possession — 'the people whose [something]'. There is no possessive relationship here; we are simply identifying who was at the demonstration.
steps.note: Full sentence: 'The people who were at the demonstration were from all sections of society.'

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is used; this question tests the correct use of the subject relative pronoun 'who' versus object form 'whom' for a human antecedent.
